analogy: Think of "World Climate and Climate Change" through the chapter's own opening images: WATER (OCEANS) This unit deals with • Hydrological Cycle • Oceans — submarine relief; distribution of temperature and salinity; movements of ocean water-waves, tides and currents UNIT V WATER (OCEANS) the ocean to land and land to ocean. The hydrological cycle describes the movement of... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
World Climate and Climate Change is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that wATER (OCEANS) This unit deals with • Hydrological Cycle • Oceans — submarine relief; distribution of temperature and salinity; movements of ocean water-waves, tides and currents UNIT V

The text explains that wATER (OCEANS) the ocean to land and land to ocean.

The text explains that the hydrological cycle describes the movement of water on, in, and above the earth.

The text explains that the water cycle has been working for billions of years and all the life on earth depends on it.

The text explains that next to air, water is the most important element required for the existence of life on earth.

The text explains that the distribution of water on earth is quite uneven.

The text explains that many locations have plenty of water while others have very limited quantity.

The text explains that the hydrological cycle, is the circulation of water within the earth’s hydrosphere in different forms i.e. the liquid, solid and the gaseous phases.

The text explains that it also refers to the continuous exchange of water between the oceans, Figure 12.1 : Hydrological Cycle Can we think of life without water?

The text explains that water is an essential component of all life forms that exist over the surface of the earth.

The text explains that the creatures on the earth are lucky that it is a water planet, otherwise we all would have no existence.

The text explains that water is a rare commodity in our solar system.

The text explains that there is no water on the sun or anywhere else in the solar system.

The text explains that the earth, fortunately has an abundant supply of water on its surface.

The text explains that hence, our planet is called the ‘Blue Planet’.

The text explains that hYDROLOGICAL CYCLE Water is a cyclic resource.

The text explains that the remaining is held as freshwater in glaciers and icecaps, groundwater sources, lakes, soil moisture, atmosphere, streams and within life.

The text explains that these features are formed, like those of the continents, by the factors of tectonic, volcanic and depositional processes.

The text explains that the continental shelves are covered with variable thicknesses of sediments brought down by rivers, glaciers, wind, from the land and distributed by waves and currents.

The text explains that massive sedimentary deposits received over a long time by the continental shelves, become the source of fossil fuels.

The text explains that they occur at the bases of continental slopes and along island arcs and are associated with active volcanoes and strong earthquakes.

The text explains that tEMPERATURE OF OCEAN WATERS This section deals with the spatial and vertical variations of temperature in various oceans.

The text explains that factors Affecting Temperature Distribution The factors which affect the distribution of temperature of ocean water are : (i) Latitude : the temperature of surface water decreases from the equator towards the poles because the amount of insolation decreases poleward.

The text explains that it results into the longitudinal variation in the temperature.

The text explains that contrary to this, the onshore winds pile up warm water near the coast and this raises the temperature.

The text explains that (iv) Ocean currents : warm ocean currents raise the temperature in cold areas while the cold currents decrease the temperature in warm ocean areas.

The text explains that gulf stream (warm current) raises the temperature near the eastern coast of North America and the West Coast of Europe while the Labrador current (cold current) lowers the temperature near the north-east coast of North America.

The text explains that all these factors influence the temperature of the ocean currents locally.

The text explains that the enclosed seas in the low latitudes record relatively higher temperature than the open seas; whereas the enclosed seas in the high latitudes have lower temperature than the open seas.

The text explains that horizontal and Vertical Distribution of Temperature The temperature-depth profile for the ocean water shows how the temperature decreases with the increasing depth.

The text explains that this boundary region, from where there is a rapid decrease of temperature, is called the thermocline.

The text explains that the temperature structure of oceans over middle and low latitudes can be described as a three-layer system from surface to the bottom.

The text explains that the first layer represents the top layer of warm oceanic water and it is about 500m thick with temperatures ranging between 20° and 25° C.

The text explains that the second layer called the thermocline layer lies below the first layer and is characterised by rapid decrease in temperature with increasing depth.

The text explains that fUNDAMENTALS OF PHYSICAL GEOGRAPHY 104 Antartic circles, the surface water temperatures are close to 0° C and so the temperature change with the depth is very slight.

The text explains that the average temperature of surface water of the oceans is about 27°C and it gradually decreases from the equator towards the poles.

The text explains that the rate of decrease of temperature with increasing latitude is generally 0.5°C per latitude.

The text explains that the average temperature is around 22°C at 20° latitudes, 14° C at 40° latitudes and 0° C near poles.

The text explains that the oceans in the northern hemisphere record relatively higher temperature than in the southern hemisphere.

The text explains that the highest temperature is not recorded at the equator but slightly towards n o r t h o f i t .

The text explains that t h e a v e r a g e a n n u a l temperatures for the northern and southern hemisphere are around 19° C and 16° C respectively.

The text explains that figure 12.4 shows the spatial pattern of surface temperature of the oceans.

The text explains that it is a well known fact that the maximum temperature of the oceans is always at their surfaces because they directly receive the heat from the sun and the heat is transmitted to the lower sections of the oceans through the process of convection.

The text explains that it results into decrease of temperature with the increasing depth, but the rate of decrease is not uniform throughout.

The text explains that the temperature falls very rapidly up to the depth of 200 m and thereafter, the rate of decrease of temperature is slowed down.

The text explains that sALINITY OF OCEAN WATERS All waters in nature, whether rain water or ocean water, contain dissolved mineral salts.

The text explains that salinity, temperature and density of water are interrelated.

The text explains that hence, any change in the temperature or density influences the salinity of water in an area.

The text explains that vertical Distribution of Salinity Salinity changes with depth, but the way it changes depends upon the location of the Figure 12.4 : Spatial pattern of surface temperature (°C) of the oceans HORIZONTAL DISTRIBUTION OF SALINITY The salinity for normal open ocean ranges between 33o/oo and 37 o/oo.

The text explains that other factors being constant, increasing salinity of seawater causes its density to increase.

The text explains that salinity, generally, increases with depth and there is a distinct zone called the halocline, where salinity increases sharply.

The text explains that high salinity seawater, generally, sinks below the lower salinity water.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Fundamentals of Physical Geography, Chapter 12: World Climate and Climate Change

cases.point: Divisions of the Ocean Floors The ocean floors can be divided into four major divisions: (i) the Continental Shelf; (ii) the Continental Slope; (iii) the Deep... (NCERT Class 11 Phys Geo Ch12 pg 2)

prelims_traps: Do not treat WATER (OCEANS) and HYDROLOGICAL CYCLE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to World Climate and Climate Change; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain World Climate and Climate Change through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use WATER (OCEANS) and HYDROLOGICAL CYCLE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
